ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'chicken soup' class (FOODON_03301521); no specific entry for Indian-style Chicken Shorba exists. Closest_parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ (Chicken soup) is the closest defensible match; no specific Shorba entry exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024. Closest_parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for Chicken Shorba identified; general food concept not clinically coded.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Chicken Shorba is approximately 250 ml (one bowl), equivalent to 250 g, providing around 163 kcal, 16.3 g protein, and 8.8 g fat.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Chicken Shorba is a traditional Indian chicken broth. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' using chicken soup / chicken broth entries as the closest approved match. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy chicken broth entry (NDB 06172) as ICMR-NIN does not disaggregate fatty acid fractions for this preparation. Monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, and trans fat data not available in either source for this preparation and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson et al.
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal, American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Chicken Shorba exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI estimated at 10 (low) by analogy with clear chicken broth / chicken soup, which contains negligible digestible carbohydrate (1.5 g per 100 g). Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (10 × 3.75 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.4, classified as very low. Estimation basis: Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables; chicken broth analogous entry.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 180 mg per 100 g; a 250 ml serving delivers 450 mg sodium, approximately 20% of the 2,000 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should consume Chicken Shorba in moderate portions and request reduced-salt preparation, as the sodium content from added salt and spice blends can accumulate significantly across a full meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (1.5 g per 100 g) and estimated GI of 10 result in a negligible glycemic load of 0.4 per serving, with high protein (6.5 g per 100 g) supporting satiety and glycemic stability.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken Shorba is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as its minimal digestible carbohydrate and high protein content produce a negligible glycemic response; it can be included freely in a diabetes meal plan provided sodium is monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat (3.5 g per 100 g) with estimated saturated fat of approximately 1.0 g per 100 g; anti-inflammatory spices (ginger, cardamom, cloves) may confer additional cardiovascular benefit.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chicken Shorba is a lean, low-calorie broth with a favourable fat profile and anti-inflammatory spice ingredients, making it a heart-friendly choice; individuals with cardiovascular disease should monitor sodium intake and avoid high-salt versions.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Ginger and other whole spices used in preparation have documented anti-inflammatory properties; chicken provides essential amino acids supporting tissue repair.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chicken Shorba may be beneficial for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the anti-inflammatory properties of ginger and aromatic spices, combined with high-quality protein from chicken to support joint tissue maintenance.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Shorba is a traditional North Indian clear chicken soup or broth prepared by simmering bone-in or boneless chicken pieces with whole aromatic spices including cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, and bay leaves, along with fresh ginger, garlic, and onions. Unlike creamy Western chicken soups, Chicken Shorba is characteristically light, clear or very lightly thickened, and deeply aromatic. The broth is finished with a tempering (tadka) of whole spices in oil or ghee, which releases fat-soluble volatile compounds and gives the soup its signature fragrance. The flavour profile is warming, savoury, and mildly spiced, with a clean finish. Texturally, the broth is thin and liquid with tender shreds of chicken. Nutritionally, Chicken Shorba is a low-calorie, high-protein food providing approximately 65 kcal and 6.5 g protein per 100 g, making it suitable for weight management, recovery from illness, and as a light starter. Its very low carbohydrate content (1.5 g per 100 g) and estimated glycemic index of 10 make it an excellent choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The sodium content (180 mg per 100 g) warrants attention for those with hypertension. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is widely consumed across North India, Pakistan, and the Indian diaspora. It is commonly served as a starter or side dish at lunch and dinner, and is traditionally recommended during recovery from cold or flu due to its warming and hydrating properties.
common_user_queries: Is Chicken Shorba good for diabetes?
How many calories in Chicken Shorba?
What spices are in Chicken Shorba?
Is Chicken Shorba good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' using the closest chicken soup/broth entry, as no exact Chicken Shorba entry exists in any approved primary source. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy chicken broth. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, cholesterol, and moisture data for Chicken Shorba are not available in ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' or USDA FoodData Central for this specific traditional preparation as of 2024. 2. Saturated fat (1.0 g per 100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy chicken broth entry (NDB 06172) as ICMR-NIN does not report disaggregated fatty acid data for this dish. 3. GI of 10 is an analogous estimate based on clear chicken broth from Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables; no direct GI measurement for Chicken Shorba exists in approved sources. 4. The input record contained three identical duplicate portion entries; deduplicated to a single standard serving of 250 g. 5. All core macros (protein_g 6.5, fat_g 3.5, carbohydrate_g 1.5) are non-zero and nutritionally plausible for a clear chicken broth preparation. 6. Halal flag set to true as chicken shorba is a traditional dish from Muslim culinary traditions in North India and Pakistan; preparation with halal-certified chicken is standard. Kosher flag set to false as mixing meat broth with potential dairy-adjacent ingredients is common in non-kosher preparation.
